title: Roadmap: load the native tree-sitter binding from a per-process temp copy — kill the EBUSY-upgrade class, no process killing ever
context: v1.3.1 rollout required manually killing 4 hunch MCP server processes that held tree-sitter.node (bug_ebusy_treesitter). Owner asked: can the install do this itself? An npm preinstall script COULD kill lockers, but a package that kills user processes on install is hostile, risks in-flight sessions, and reads as supply-chain malware — off-brand for a trust-centered tool. The lock itself is the bug, not the killing.
decision: Load the tree-sitter native from a per-process TEMP COPY (copy tree-sitter.node to tmpdir at load, require from there — the esbuild pattern for exactly this Windows problem). The installed file is then never memory-locked, so npm i -g always succeeds with sessions open; running servers keep serving the old code until their natural restart. Supersedes the lazy-load idea on bug_ebusy_treesitter (lazy only delays the lock until first reindex; tmp-copy removes it). Interim UX: a hunch upgrade command that finds hunch-owned MCP processes by exact package path, says what it found, and only kills with an explicit --kill — the dance behind a consented command with a receipt, never a hidden install script.
consequences: Upgrades stop caring about open sessions on Windows
tmpdir gets one small .node copy per server process (sweep like hookcache)
alternatives_rejected: npm preinstall that kills locking processes: hidden process-killing on install is hostile + supply-chain-scary; also races the file copy
Lazy-loading tree-sitter (the earlier candidate): the MCP server reindexes on queries, so the lock returns after first use — delays, not solves
Documenting close-first forever: works (the cheat sheet says it) but every Windows user pays it on every upgrade
